query: USCIS reached the fiscal year 2004 H-1B cap and stopped accepting new first-time employment petitions.
Current H-1B workers could still extend status, change employers, or work concurrently after the cap was reached.
H-1B petitions for institutions of higher education and nonprofit research organizations were exempt from the annual cap.
DHS stopped accepting H-2B petitions for fiscal year 2004 after the numerical limit was reached.
The H-2B numerical limitation was 66,000 workers for a fiscal year.
H-2B petitions for work beginning on or after October 1, 2004 were not affected by the fiscal year 2004 cap procedures.
In fiscal year 2002, computer-related occupations made up a smaller share of H-1B approvals than in 2001.
Most H-1B beneficiaries in fiscal year 2002 had at least a bachelor's degree, and many had graduate degrees.
The U.S. labor market showed signs of recovery by the end of 2003, with temporary help services adding workers.
Mortgage rates and housing demand supported employment growth in construction and real estate in 2003.
Demand for H-1B professionals rises and falls with economic conditions instead of staying constant at the cap.
Department of Labor findings showed only a small number of willful H-1B violations in 2001 and 2002.
